data.title: 12 Years With Zero Gains: Can Your Retirement Plan Survive A 'Lost Decade?'
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

It's the holiday season, a time when we get together with family and friends. Often, as we get together, we share stories about times we've shared in the past. Something funny happens as the years go by: The stories evolve.

Even though we're talking about the same shared experience, little details will change. Some details are forgotten, others are emphasized, and our memory of the event can actually change.

We might remember the main events accurately, but we might be completely wrong on the details and not even know it. In a holiday setting, this often means that we remember the good things.

Though for some, the bad memories of holidays can overwhelm the good ones. Those who have lost a loved one, gone through a traumatic event, or have broken ties in their family might go the opposite direction and focus on the negative memories. For those of you reading this who just wish the holiday season would be over – I understand how you feel.

When it comes to the market, our memories can be similarly selective and similarly inaccurate, or at least not a complete picture of what happened.

Historical Perspective

When it comes to investing, it's easy to forget the messy times. We talk about investing in the stock market, and this is what we remember:

Look at those little pullbacks in the early 2000s and in 2009, barely a blip! Time has a way of making old swings look so small. The reality was much worse.

Yes, that's three years during which the stock market went down. For three years, investors in the S&P 500 lost money every year. Losses during the GFC were steeper, but happened faster:

These dips that look so benign on the charts today were serious issues for many retirees. Not all of them made it through with their portfolios. Retirees who were dependent upon selling shares to fund their retirement were faced with the reality that selling would cripple their portfolio. Many retirement plans were destroyed during these periods.

In contrast, the COVID crash was scary in its ferocity, but it was brief.

Look Through the Lens of Reality

With 15 years since the last sustained crash in the stock markets, there are a lot of investors who haven't been through one. As I'm talking to people, many of them seem to feel invincible. They have bought into the myth that the market should go up +15%/year and that this is normal and sustainable. I think many can't imagine going three, five, or even 10 years without having a positive total return in the stock market.

Yet, it has happened. From Jan. 1, 2000, through Dec. 31, 2011, the S&P 500 had a total return of 5.93%.

Can your retirement plan sustain that kind of extended period without capital gains?

The Value of Cash Flow

Consider the following backtest. With 12 holdings from the High Dividend Opportunities Model Portfolio, selected based on existing in 2001 and today.

We included some holdings like Greystone Housing Investors (GHI) and Macerich (MAC) have seen prices come down a lot, and holdings like Realty Income (O) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which have seen a lot of upside. Plus a lot of holdings that have been somewhere in between.

Overall, one-third of the holdings outperformed SPY, one-third materially underperformed with a CAGR below 6% and one-third were slightly lower and in the ballpark:

We aren't going to give away our whole portfolio in one article, but we approached it with every holding in our portfolio that was also trading in January 2001.

We will start from January 2002 because, since then, the total return of the two portfolios has been close to the same. The S&P 500 outperformed slightly at 9.7% vs. 9.45%, good for about a quarter million dollars:

Oh no, we bought the losers! But wait, what happens if a person is actually withdrawing cash?

Let's look at what happens when you include annual cash withdrawals. We'll start with 4% year one and adjust it for inflation each year after. So, with a portfolio starting out with $500k, the investor is withdrawing $20k in year one. Same portfolios, same starting date, and same ending date. The only difference from the scenario above is withdrawing $20k/year in income:

Suddenly, Team HDO goes from being behind to outperforming. The total return is $800k higher. The reason this happens is because the S&P 500 didn't have a positive total return for many years. As a result, the investor in SPY was selling shares at poor prices to fund the withdrawals. Meanwhile, Team HDO was producing over $40k/year in dividends, more than enough to fund the withdrawals.

While the investment in SPY was selling shares, Team HDO was buying shares. This is a positive when share prices are lower than they will be in the future.

What If You Withdraw More?

Let's face it, $20k/year isn't withdrawing much relative to the amount of work it is to build a retirement portfolio that's $500,000. Financial advisors frequently suggest withdrawing 4% maximum because that helps ensure your portfolio will survive extended downturns. In all fairness, that rule worked, even buying in 2001 when the market was relatively high and on the cusp of a significant downturn.

With the Income Method, we suggest withdrawing 75% of your dividends and reinvesting 25%. How would such an aggressive withdrawal work out?

For SPY, not well at all. Withdrawing $32,000/year and adjusting for inflation, a SPY investor would be down to $3,500 in December 2022:

Team HDO is humming along with an ending balance of over $1 million (on $500k starting) and still producing ample income to cover current withdrawals:

When you start accounting for withdrawals, suddenly, historical performance starts looking a lot different. When people are measuring "total return," more often than not, they're assuming that no money is ever withdrawn. That isn't the reality for retirees.

Let's Be Aware of Our Own Myth

Let's not be guilty of ignoring our own history. That decline of income in 2009 doesn't look like a big deal in the charts, but it was certainly scary. In 2009 and 2010, the scheduled withdrawal would have been more than $39k, while the portfolio produced dividends of $40k.

With the 25% cushion gone, the investor should have been considering trimming back the withdrawals if possible. With 20/20 hindsight, we know that the dividends recovered and the cushion was over 25% again by 2012, even with full withdrawals. However, in the moment, that couldn't be known.

It isn't that our investment strategy is magically immune to challenges. However, one benefit is that it's quantifiable. This investor in 2009 knows that their cushion is gone and can take relatively small adjustments to reduce their spending and have a little bit larger cushion. The investor with SPY was selling nearly $30k of stock per year before the crisis started. So, how much do they need to reduce spending to protect their portfolio?

That can be calculated in hindsight, but in the moment, an investor would have no idea how much they could safely withdraw.

Conclusion

The strength of our investing strategy isn't that it will always have the highest total return. Sometimes it will, sometimes it won't. It isn't that you will be able to withdraw a fixed amount of income with absolute certainty for the rest of your life; there will be periods where dividend cuts are frequent, and income will decline. Your portfolio might not produce the income you expected it to, and you might have to make changes.

The strength of the Income Method is that it gives us a real-time guide to how much income our portfolio is producing right now. That amount will change. Over the long run, it will rise, and we do our best to actively manage our portfolio to help it rise faster. However, there will be challenges. Our approach gives you the tools to manage those challenges with clear goals and clear warning signals.

You don't have to guess. If your income goes up, you know it went up. If it goes down, you know it went down the moment it happens. You don't have to guess how much you can withdraw, because you know exactly how much income your portfolio produced before you take any cash out.

That's the Income Method.

data.title: Why Apple's Momentum Isn't Accidental
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Apple’s (AAPL) stock has climbed roughly 15% since my last coverage, and the momentum still leans toward its fast-growing, high-margin Services segment. Services revenue jumped 15% YoY to $28.8 billion, widening its lead over overall growth and reducing reliance on hardware volatility. A 2.35 billion-device ecosystem, strong iPhone 17 demand, and elite profitability help fund Apple’s aggressive AI CapEx. Despite valuation and FCF concerns, this structural shift toward recurring revenue continues to anchor the bullish case.

Services Revenue Surges 15% to Propel Apple’s Growth

Currently, I see the most supporting factor for Apple’s stock price growth is the aggressive expansion of its high-margin Services segment. The segment is successfully monetizing a massive and loyal installed base through hardware advancement. As per the quant profitability grade, Apple holds a Net Income Margin (TTM) of 26.92% that is vastly superior to the sector median of 4.86%. This represents a percentage difference in the sector of 454%.

I believe this high profitability is the result of a structural shift in revenue composition. The Q4 results pointed to this trend specifically. While total revenue grew 8% YoY to $102.5 billion, Services revenue grew at a much faster pace of 15% YoY. It is reaching an all-time record of $28.8 billion. This gap indicates that Apple is becoming less dependent on unit volume sales of hardware for profitability.

Additionally, this factor is connected with the relationship between Apple's hardware cycle and its software ecosystem. Apple’s installed base has surpassed 2.35 billion users, and this massive figure acts as a multiplier for the Services segment. Apple is actively stimulating this base with new hardware releases like the iPhone 17 lineup and the introduction of the M5 chip. iPhone revenue for the September quarter was $46.2 billion, but forward supply chain data suggests strong demand for the iPhone 17 as sales in China surged 22% in the first month post-launch (with improving annual sales growth).

This hardware success is vital because it refreshes the installed base with devices capable of running advanced features like Apple Intelligence. Now, as users upgrade to these AI-capable devices, they may become more attached to the ecosystem that secured the 15% topline growth of the Services segment.

Further, the sharpness with which Apple manages the capital is seen in its Return on Common Equity (ROCE). Apple’s TTM ROCE is 171.42% against the sector median’s 6.07%. This 27 times difference against the sector points out that for every dollar of equity shareholder capital, Apple derives much higher profit than its peers.

This bottom-line element allows Apple to self-fund the massive CapEx needed to stabilize this growth without eroding stock value. The YoY CapEx growth of 34.59% is way higher than the sector median of 11.24%. This heavy investment is directed toward AI infra and private cloud computing. By aggressively reinvesting capital (high CAPEX growth) into AI capabilities, Apple can future-proof its high-margin Services moat.

To me, the forward trend suggests that this bottom-line strength will compound over the long term. Apple is moving toward a potential deal with Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) to integrate Gemini models that may be costing ~$1 billion annually. With that, it is simultaneously building out its own proprietary AI infrastructure. While this increases operating expenses based largely on research, it boosts the value proposition of Apple’s hardware in the market. An Asset Turnover Ratio of 1.15x compared to the sector's 0.61x indicates that despite the heavy asset base required for hardware manufacturing and data centers, Apple is still highly efficient at generating revenue from its asset base.

Overall, Apple's core advantage lies in its capability to utilize its return on equity to fund a 34.59% increase in CAPEX. Thereby, Apple is driving a hardware supercycle (iPhone 17) that feeds a record-breaking, high-margin Services business. This self-reinforcing cycle differentiates Apple financials from sector volatility and supports continued stock price appreciation despite macro pressures.

The Hidden Risks Lurking in Apple’s Valuation

At the same time, the downside factor that I am monitoring for Apple stock is the widening disconnect between its premium valuation multiples and a considerable deterioration in its FCF growth. The issue is also compounded by escalating regulatory threats. Apple stock is trading at a P/E Non-GAAP (forward) of 33.83x, which is a 43.16% premium over the sector median of 23.63x. This earns Apple a valuation grade of D+.

Even more concerning is the Price/Book ratio of 46.09x, graded as an F, which represents a staggering 967.13% premium over the sector median of 4.32x. These metrics mean that the Street has priced the stock for flawless exponential growth. However, the growth metrics contradict the justification for such a high premium, mainly on liquidity.

Additionally, the growth metrics of Apple have a severe contraction in cash flow efficiency. Levered FCF Growth (YoY) is at -28.85% with a grade of D. This is in contrast to the sector median that grew at 7.66%. A negative FCF growth rate of ~29% hurts the primary thesis for high valuation Apple stock. This compression is likely a direct result of the surge in CapEx that grew at 34.59% YoY. This spending is necessary for AI infrastructure, but the immediate financial impact is a reduction in the free cash available for buybacks and dividends compared to previous years.

The PEG non-GAAP (forward) ratio of 3.28 also further shows this weakness. A PEG ratio above 1 typically suggests overvaluation, and Apple’s 3.28 (against the sector median of 1.69) indicates that the stock price is outpacing its expected growth rate by a factor of three. With that, the Street is paying a massive premium for growth that, in terms of FCF, is currently negative.

This valuation risk is also expanded by distinct forward regulatory threats that hurt the high-margin Services revenue needed to justify these multiples. A potential fine of up to $38 billion in India related to antitrust penalty laws concerning global turnover.

Additionally, the European Union's Digital Markets Act (DMA) continues to pressure Apple to open its ecosystem that creates risks of fraud and scams. However, more fundamentally, it threatens Apple’s commission structure of the App Store. Apple has already been hit with a €500 million fine and faces continued scrutiny that can force a reduction in the 30% commission that supports the Services segment's high margins.

If regulatory bodies succeed in dismantling the exclusivity of Apple’s payment and app distribution systems, the Net Income Margin of 26.92% may face compression. Apple stock's current valuation (P/E 37.33) is based on the consistency of these monopolistic-style margins. However, the reality of shrinking Levered FCF (-28.85%) combined with the external threat of multi-billion dollar fines and forced business model changes creates an adverse scenario.

Overall, if the Services topline growth (currently 15%) slows due to regulatory intervention and if the heavy AI CapEx fails to yield immediate returns, the PEG ratio of 3.28 will likely revert toward the sector median. As a result, this reversion may lead to a considerable downward correction in the stock price. So, this valuation-to-cash-flow disconnect is a primary weakness for Apple’s stock price in my opinion.

Takeaway

Apple’s 15% surge since my last coverage underscores how its accelerating Services engine is reshaping the company’s financial core. As the firm sees record levels of margin, an increasingly extensive AI ecosystem, and an updated product lineup, the fundamental growth potential for the entire concern remains locked into this ongoing reinvention.

data.title: CoreWeave: Sell Before It All Falls Apart
data.description:
In the last month, CoreWeave’s (CRWV) shares depreciated by around 30% and are unlikely to rebound to their highs in the foreseeable future, as the number of risks that the company faces has significantly increased and now limits its upside. Even though CoreWeave was able to become one of the largest neocloud providers on the market, its reliance on debt financing, rising interest expenses, and record capital expenditures on depreciating GPUs could undermine the bullish thesis. Because of that and the fact that CoreWeave’s shares remain overvalued even after the latest depreciation, we believe that giving the company the rating of Sell makes the most sense at this stage.

The Risks Are Significant

CoreWeave specializes in building large-scale GPU clusters that are later rented to other companies that use them to execute their AI projects. Thanks to the rise of generative AI in recent years, the demand for such a service has skyrocketed, and in Q3 alone, CoreWeave’s revenues increased by 132.9% Y/Y to $1.36 billion. In addition, the company has secured numerous multibillion-dollar commitments from companies like Meta Platforms and OpenAI, which helped it to increase its revenue backlog by 271% Y/Y to $55.6 billion at the end of Q3.

The reason why tech behemoths rent GPU clusters from neocloud providers like CoreWeave is that they receive financial flexibility from such services. Those tech companies have access to additional computing power at a time when there’s an increased demand for their AI projects without the need to build and maintain new data centers to meet that demand.

The costs of running those data centers under such arrangements fall entirely on companies like CoreWeave. Because of the expanding backlog, CoreWeave constantly needs to spend excessive sums of money to increase its computing power, which affects the performance of the overall business. If we look at the company’s cash flow statement, we’ll see that in 2023 its CapEx was $2.94 billion, while in 2024 it already increased to $8.7 billion. Considering that CoreWeave had around 2.9 gigawatts in contracted power thanks to various commitments at the end of Q3, while its active power was only around 590 megawatts, we could expect an even greater increase in capital expenditures in the following years. During the latest earnings call, CoreWeave’s management already stated that their 2025 CapEx is expected to be in the range of $12 billion to $14 billion, while in 2026, it’s expected to be well in excess of double that of this year.

The problem is that despite the massive expansion of its business, CoreWeave remains unprofitable, and there’s no guarantee that it will break even anytime soon. In Q3, its net loss was $110 million. Since most organizations get zero return on their AI investments, while companies like Oracle are struggling with improving their margins when renting Nvidia’s GPUs, there’s a risk that CoreWeave won’t become profitable anytime soon.

What’s worse is that as the business is losing money, CoreWeave continues to increase its debt burden, making it even harder for the company to improve its bottom-line performance and reach a breakeven point. After all, in Q3, CoreWeave paid $310.56 million in interest expenses, an increase from $104.38 million a year ago. At the same time, its total debt at the end of Q3 was $14.2 billion, up from $8 billion at the end of 2024. The company also expanded its debt facility last month, which shows that it’s unlikely to stop relying on the debt financing options to run the business.

Another problem is that a significant portion of funds is spent buying advanced GPUs, primarily from Nvidia, which fairly quickly depreciate in value. CoreWeave itself, in its S-1 filing, noted that the useful life of its technology equipment is only six years. In addition to that, as more powerful chips are introduced, there’s a risk that the useful lives of GPUs could be even shorter. After all, Nvidia said that it plans to release new AI accelerators each year. It already expects the release of chips from the Rubin series next year, even though the ramp-up production of its chips from the Blackwell series began less than a year ago. As such, there’s a risk that CoreWeave will be left holding outdated GPUs on which it currently spends impressive sums of money, and will be required to constantly spend additional resources on new GPUs to expand its data center footprint and remain competitive. Because of that, we believe that the risks that CoreWeave faces are significant and won’t disappear anytime soon.

The Real Value of CoreWeave

Considering all of those risks, we decided to create a valuation model and find out the real value of CoreWeave. The perpetual growth rate in the model is 3%, which we constantly use to value other companies. The cash, total debt, and share count data that were used in the model have been taken from the recent 10-Q report.

The discount rate in the model is 10.58%. We figured it out by calculating CoreWeave’s cost of debt and cost of equity. When calculating the cost of debt, we used the total debt data from the latest 10-Q report and the interest expense data from the trailing twelve months. To calculate the cost of equity, we used a risk-free rate of 4.10%, a beta of 2.13, and the market return rate of 7.77%.

We also assume a triple-digit sales growth rate in 2025, after which we expect the gradual normalization of the growth rate. Our assumption is also close to the overall consensus, and it’s more than possible for the company to achieve an aggressive growth rate in the following years, considering that its backlog at the end of Q3 was over $55 billion. We also expect the gradual improvement of EBIT margins as the business scales and becomes more efficient, while the tax rate in our model is 21%, which is the current standard corporate tax rate in the United States.

When it comes to CapEx, the management said that they expect their expenditures to be in the range of $12 billion to $14 billion in 2025, and well in excess of double that range in 2026. We reflected that in the model as well. We also assume a higher depreciation & amortization in the following years due to the expected depreciation of GPUs that CoreWeave acquired and will continue to buy in the future. The change in net working capital in our model is positive in the upcoming years.

With those assumptions, our model shows that CoreWeave’s enterprise value is $31.54 billion, while its equity value is only $19.23 billion due to the significant debt burden. As a result, our model shows that CoreWeave’s intrinsic value is $38.63 per share, which is around 50% below the current market price.

Risks to Our Bearish Thesis

There’s a possibility that at the upcoming FOMC meeting later this month, the Federal Reserve decides to further cut the interest rates, which could be viewed positively by the market. If that happens, CoreWeave’s stock could rebound and trade at higher levels despite being greatly overvalued.

In addition, if hyperscalers continue to increase their spending to expand their AI infrastructure, then CoreWeave could greatly increase its backlog and improve its overall top-line performance. If that happens, then CoreWeave’s shares could also appreciate in the foreseeable future and undermine our bearish thesis for a while.

Final Thoughts

While CoreWeave’s stock could rebound along with the rest of the market if the overall macro environment improves, we believe that any momentum the shares could get will be short-lived. After all, the company is currently stuck in an endless cycle of increasing its capital expenditures that exceed its revenues to buy quickly depreciating AI accelerators in order to meet the rising backlog. As it’s doing all of that, its total debt increases, while the interest expenses are rising and are already negatively affecting the company’s financial performance. Because of that, it’s hard to find value in CoreWeave’s shares, especially at the current price. If the AI bubble risks materialize and the macro environment worsens, then a further decline of the company’s shares will likely be a matter of time. For that reason, we rate CoreWeave as a Sell.

data.title: The One AI Chart That Completely Reshaped My Portfolio
data.description:
As anyone who is paying attention to financial markets knows by now, artificial intelligence ("AI") has taken the stock market by storm over the past several years, with the likes of NVIDIA (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR) delivering phenomenal total returns over that period.

This is due to the fact that the market is currently pricing in rather bullish expectations for how dramatically this new technology will transform the global economy in the coming years and decades.

While some fear that AI may eventually become fully sentient and ultimately go rogue and potentially even subjugate the human race, less dramatic concerns are still quite serious, in particular the concern that AI will displace most members of the U.S. workforce. For example, Elon Musk has cautioned that AI will likely make work largely optional and usher in a massive deflationary boom. In this article, I will look at this concern and share my thoughts on how it could potentially impact the stock market and how I am allocating capital today.

The Most Exposed Job Categories

AI is rapidly advancing the ability to automate not only simple but increasingly complex tasks, especially in the white-collar and knowledge-intensive sectors of the economy, including finance, healthcare, professional services, and even STEM fields, especially computer coding. The most exposed areas are categories like Human Resources, Logistics, Office Administration, and Financial Analysis.

This will likely lead to a major reduction in labor costs per unit of output and thereby have a dramatic deflationary impact on the economy. In fact, a recent MIT study revealed that even AI in its current form is already capable of replacing a workload equivalent to about 11.7% of the current U.S. labor market, which is about $1.2 trillion worth of wages. This shows that AI is already a major disruptive force in the labor market and could be even more disruptive in the years to come as agentic AI and robotics take off.

The labor market is not keeping up. This seems to indicate there is a growing and likely permanent divergence between corporate output and profitability and demand for human labor. In fact, a recent study revealed that since ChatGPT launched, job openings are down by over 30%, while the stock market is up by 75%.

This trend should have a deflationary impact on wages and will also likely increasingly concentrate wealth in the hands of businesses that are able to successfully implement AI to drive business efficiencies without losing market share to competition.

At the same time, though, the reduced need for labor and the rapid and cost-efficient scaling capabilities of robotics and AI will likely push more and more people to start their own businesses instead of pursuing a traditional nine-to-five, multi-decade career working for a big corporation. This will lead to more competition, a more nimble and tailored economy in which businesses are increasingly applying AI models with careful tailoring filters to solve specific problems, thereby making products much more efficient and effective in solving consumer problems while also coming to market at much cheaper pricing due to the lower labor needs to produce them.

The Winners and Losers of the AI Revolution

So what does this mean for the markets and capital allocation as a whole? First of all, it means that there will likely be some enormously successful winners. We have already seen this begin with some of the leading megacaps and even other AI-powered upstarts like PLTR in recent years.

That said, virtually all of this, with the exception of companies like NVDA and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which are truly printing cash as fundamental AI infrastructure providers, as well as energy infrastructure companies that are being leaned heavily on to provide the needed power to fuel the AI boom via data centers, is largely due to expectations about the future rather than current profits. For example, companies like Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), and others are currently being bid up very aggressively with expectations that they are going to be the AI winners of tomorrow, largely due to the fact that they are investing immense sums of money today to prepare to lay the groundwork for that future.

However, their success is far from certain, and if their AI ventures fail to deliver profits on their mass investments, they may simply be later viewed as the sacrificial lambs who deployed the capital needed to advance AI models and to build out the infrastructure needed to bring in the fourth industrial revolution. Nevertheless, the true profits may be built on top of their ashes by future startups or even consumer goods and industrial businesses that tailor their core AI infrastructure and models to their specific industries to dramatically improve their profits.

Why I Am Avoiding The Highly Valued S&P 500

Given the uncertainties facing these companies and the massive amounts of capital they are outlaying right now to even begin to approach the long-term potential of AI, the risk–reward is not entirely favorable. In particular, this is seen in the fact that the S&P 500 (SPY) by virtually any major metric, is significantly overvalued right now, with these megacap AI tech stocks increasingly dominating the index.

This makes the S&P 500 relatively unattractive from a risk–reward profile today because you are paying the cost of these stocks that already have much of the future promise of AI priced into them, while there is still significant downside if either AI as a whole fails to achieve its potential or, at the very least, if these leading megacap companies fail to realize their potential.

Therefore, I am steering clear of leading market indexes like SPY and QQQ and instead selectively investing in companies that should be relatively resistant to AI disruptive forces, whether it be jobs getting wiped out or an AI technology race to develop the best AI model, and instead am putting the vast majority of my capital into essential infrastructure that will be needed and valued regardless of how exactly the AI boom turns out.

Where I Am Investing Right Now

In particular, I am finding a lot of attractive opportunities in midstream infrastructure businesses (AMLP) that trade on a value basis today with high yields, strong growth rates, and stellar balance sheets, while having highly contracted cash flows and should enjoy relatively stable to growing demand whether it be from AI data center demand, LNG exports, or simply general economic activity as the U.S. pivots towards reindustrialization.

One of my favorite opportunities at the moment is ONEOK (OKE) as it has significant data center natural gas deal exposure while also being well diversified across other lines of business. Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) is another opportunity I like over the long term, as it is also a well-diversified infrastructure company, including midstream that should benefit from AI demand, utilities that should benefit from AI demand, a well-diversified transportation business that has high cash visibility well into the future, and a digital infrastructure including a data center business that is also directly benefiting from AI demand. Finally, I also like Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC) as a diversified power production company that includes nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, and battery storage and is enjoying very strong growth, including recent AI power-providing agreements with leading hyperscalers like META and GOOGL.

Investor Takeaway

While the future of AI is uncertain and its potential ramifications on the economy are immense due to its ability to displace a lot of employees, the long-term impacts of this are likely to be such that there is going to be a startup boom.

This startup boom is going to have one of two effects that are going to impact the economy in significant ways. On the one hand, AI is going to enable some of the best-positioned large companies to completely dominate their areas, while the AI boom is also going to unleash a tidal wave of competitive pressures in industries where a moat is less likely to be achieved, which would drive costs materially lower. Additionally, simply to get to that point, there are immense capital investments needed, which leading megacap tech stocks are making today. It is also quite probable that at least several of them will end up not achieving even close to comparable returns relative to what they are laying out in capital today, given how richly valued that group of stocks is in aggregate today, especially when looking at historic valuation multiples for market indexes relative to where they are today. I think that this makes SPY a pretty risky bet, especially due to the likely disruption from job losses and a massive wave of startups hitting the economy.

Instead, I am placing my bets on AI-adjacent infrastructure plays where the future is much more certain and total return potential is quite attractive. This is where I am focusing my investments today at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Science Applications International: Growing Margins And Robust Return On Equity Signal Strength
data.description:
Investment thesis

On the morning of December 4, 2025, Science Applications International Corporation (SAIC) released its Q3-fiscal 2026 earnings release (its fiscal years end on the Friday closest to January 31—fiscal 2026 began on February 1, 2025).

Investors liked what they saw and bid the price up 16.29% that day, from $87.53 to $101.79. What’s more, that increase mostly held the following day, declining just 1.16%.

What did investors see that they liked? I argue it is news of margin growth, both in the past and continuing in coming years.

About SAIC

The company called itself a ‘mission integrator’ in its 10-K for fiscal 2025; it provides “technical, engineering and enterprise information technology ("IT") services primarily to the U.S. government.”

It operates through two segments: (1) Defense and Intelligence and (2) Civilian. In the first nine months of fiscal 2026, Defense and Intelligence accounted for $4.246 billion (77%) of its revenue, while Civilian accounted for $1.266 billion (23%).

And that’s where the fiscal 2025 and 2026 story begins, with the election of a new administration in November 2024 and potential federal spending cuts. Since the bulk of SAIC’s revenue comes from federal agencies, shareholders began to find the exits and the share price began falling, albeit in an off-and-on way:

As this chart shows, most of the gains built up over the previous four years melted away in the year following the election; the stock lost close to a third of its value from the peak.

Meanwhile, the fundamentals were on a different path. This three-year chart of price and net income shows how the latter peaked in the third quarter of fiscal 2023, slumped for several quarters, and then began recovering in the third quarter of fiscal 2024:

With that context, we can assess its third-quarter results.

Q3-2025 results

These were the key results, as provided in the report, with Q3-2024 results in brackets:

  • Revenue: $1.866 billion ($1.976 billion)
  • Operating income: $128 million ($160 million)
  • Net income: $78 million ($106 million)
  • Basic EPS: $1.70 ($2.15)
  • Diluted EPS: $1.69 ($2.13).

Across the board, then, results were below those of the same quarter last year. There is certainly nothing that would appear to justify a price increase of 16.29% on the day the earnings were released.

Guidance for the full-year was mixed, with several key metrics showing improvement this fiscal year and in fiscal 2027, which begins February 1st. SAIC provided this set of targets in its Q3-fiscal 2026 earnings presentation:

Data does not include potential gains from its latest acquisition, SilverEdge, in October.

Again, I don’t see much that would push the price that much higher on the day of the release. While we can see gains in several areas for this year and next, there’s nothing outstanding here.

SAIC margins

What I do find encouraging about the company, though, is its improving net income and net income margin:

If we consider the big jump In Q2-fiscal 2023 and the decline back to 'normal' as a temporary aberration, then it is plain net income and the net income margin have risen significantly over the past five years. Not smoothly, but certainly growing.

Between fiscal 2021 and TTM fiscal 2026, net income has risen from $211 million to $371 million, an increase of 75.83%. That is encouraging information.

And more bottom-line improvement appears to be coming. On the Q3 earnings call, Interim CEO and Director James Reagan said, “We have identified over $100 million in annual spend that we're actively working to reinvest into higher ROI areas across our business and increase margins. This should result in a more efficient SAIC with increased investment directly driving growth and margins approaching 10% in the near term with additional potential upside in FY '27 as we drive further efficiency across the business.”

In other words, moving into areas with higher returns on investment will increase margins, making more cash available for growth investments. But, that’s already been happening, with both retained earnings and return on equity increasing significantly in the past five years:

Retained earnings for Q3-fiscal 2026 are not yet available, but will be once SAIC files its 10-Q for the quarter.

Investors will focus on the ROE, which is currently 23.75%. To put that figure in context, here are returns on equity for a few of its peers and/or competitors:

  • FTI Consulting, Inc. (FCN): 13.30%
  • Planet Labs PBC (PL): -19.90%
  • Exponent, Inc. (EXPO): 25.79%
  • Industrials sector median: 12.43%
  • S&P 500 average since 1957: 10.54% (according to Investopedia).

While Exponent does slightly better, SAIC outperforms FTI, Planet Labs, and the Industrials sector median by a wide margin.

From another perspective, using the Rule of 72 tells us a return of 23.75% will double in slightly more than three years. That’s a very attractive proposition if that rate can be maintained.

SAIC prospects

EPS estimates by the Wall Street analysts are relatively bullish:

Perhaps share prices will keep rising, as investors see better margins and earnings, and if the current economic uncertainty fades.

Valuation and price targets

In the past year, the stock price has fallen 17.85%, although it recovered somewhat after the Q3 earnings report was released:

Given that decline, along with improvements in net income and earnings, I would expect SAIC to be undervalued, and that is the message in the Seeking Alpha valuation table:

Note that the P/E values are well below the Industrials sector medians.

The undervaluation thesis is backed by an intrinsic value calculation provided by OmniCalculator:

The inputs are: the company’s midpoint estimate of fiscal 2026 adjusted diluted EPS; the average EPS gains forecast by the Wall Street analysts; Moody's Seasoned Aaa Corporate Bond Yield for December 4; and the closing price on December 5, 2025.

Wall Street analysts have an average one-year price target of $114.63, a 13.93% increase on the December 5 closing price. Their estimates range from a low of $94.00 to a high of $130.00. Along with the estimates come one Strong Buy rating and seven Hold ratings.

These analysts have a good track record when their one-year targets are compared to actual prices:

Nevertheless, I believe the price will range between $115.00 and $120.00. That is based on not only the growing earnings through margin improvement, but also the discrepancy between the directions of earnings and price.

As noted above, if adjusted diluted earnings per share reach the midpoint of $9.90, then the bottom line will have increased 8.43% while the price has fallen 17.85% (the two time periods are not synchronous, but the comparison makes the point). Therefore, I think there may be pricing catch-up as well as earnings-driven price increases.

Given that estimated price range, the high ROE, and ongoing efforts to improve margins, I will maintain the Buy rating I posted on February 20, 2025, “Time To Look At Science Applications International In A Positive Light Again”. There have been no other Seeking Alpha analyst ratings in the past 90 days, and the Quant system has a Hold rating.

Risk factors

SAIC depends heavily on U.S. government contracts for its revenues. Although unlikely, the government could cut spending. Or, the firm’s work may not satisfy the government agencies, which would harm both its reputation and its financial stability.

The industry is highly competitive, and awarded on the basis of bids submitted by vendors. If it is unable to control its costs or unable to bid effectively, it could lose material amounts of its revenue.

My assumptions, as well as those of management and other analysts may turn out to be incorrect. As a result, following our advice could lead to adverse outcomes. Every investor should do their own due diligence to help ensure there are no surprises or at least minimize surprises.

The company depends on the expertise of its employees and may struggle to find appropriately prepared staff. In addition, many of its employees require security clearances and it may not be able to recruit as readily as it needs.

As a defense and intelligence contractor to the government, SAIC is subject to rules and regulations, routine and surprise investigations, and other oversight. Failure to comply with the many different criteria set by the government could hurt its top and bottom lines.

Conclusion

I believe investors were right to buy and push up the SAIC price after the earnings release on December 4. With growing margins, a company has greater earnings and more opportunities to keep building organically and through acquisitions.

Higher net income leads to higher returns on equity, and SAIC has an excellent ROE. That tells us the company is well managed and likely to keep growing profitably in the future.

Based on these factors, I am maintaining my Buy rating on SAIC.

data.title: PennantPark Floating Rate Capital: Very Good Moment To Exit (Downgrade)
data.description:
PennantPark Floating Rate Capital (PFLT) has experienced decent volatility this year. So far, on a total return basis, PFLT is down ~3%, which is largely in line with the sector average (BIZD):

However, from the price performance perspective, PFLT has dropped by about 13%, which has, in turn, supported the formation of a discount to NAV. Currently, PFLT trades at a P/NAV of 0.81x - i.e., a discount of 19% to the underlying NAV. Meanwhile, the BDC sector average P/NAV is 0.83x.

In my opinion, this is not fair. Namely, looking at PFLT's fundamentals, I do not see a justified reason for such a pricing. For example, what strikes me the most is that PennantPark Investment Corp. (PNNT), which is a BDC managed by the same ultimate money managers, has almost the exact same multiple (P/NAV value) as PFLT. The thing is that PNNT's leverage is one of the highest in the entire sector, its NII per share is deep below the base dividend, and the recent rate of change in the NII generation has been nothing less than very bad. In other words, PFLT has better financials across the board, but the valuation is almost equal to that of PNNT.

In my previous piece on PFLT that was issued right after the Q3, 2025 earnings release (representing the Q2 calendar period), I plotted a rather bullish case on this BDC, recognizing basically the same situation that I described above. Since then, PFLT has returned roughly 5% in total returns. But, the same could be said for BIZD (although, not PNNT, which has declined by ~10%).

Let's now take a closer look at the most recent financials to understand how PFLT's equity story looks now and whether the bull case really remains intact.

Thesis review

In Q4, 2025, PFLT delivered core net investment income per share of $0.28, which is $0.01 above the result achieved in the prior quarter.

The improvement was primarily stimulated by the larger portfolio base, where the positive effect of it was strong enough to even offset the headwinds from lower base rates, which for most BDCs has led to weaker NII results.

In the meantime, the portfolio quality has remained robust. For example, the share of income that is produced by PIK is only 1.8%. The portfolio's median leverage ratio for portfolio loan investments is still very conservative - 4.5x. The same applies to median interest coverage ratios, which stand at 2x. But, basically, I think that the non-accrual statistic tells the story well. PFLT ended the quarter with a non-accrual rate of 0.2% (based on FV), which is definitely one of the best metrics in the entire sector.

So, when it comes to earnings direction and portfolio quality, PFLT is in a solid position, which, of course, provides a solid basis for making an argument that PFLT should be trading at a richer multiple than 0.83x (sector average).

Finally, what could strengthen the story here even further is the fact that PFLT, since its inception, has invested $596 million in equity stakes, generating an IRR of 25% and a multiple of 2x on invested capital. Given that the M&A markets are opening up, the rates are lower and likely headed downwards, and there is relatively material equity exposure that sits inside of PFLT's total asset base, we could make an argument that in the foreseeable future PFLT should benefit from attractive equity monetization events (i.e., exits).

However, I will explain why, despite these positives, I have liquidated my position in PFLT. There are a couple of points.

As mentioned earlier, the reason why PFLT's core NII per share increased was because of a portfolio expansion. Since PFLT's dividend is already underfunded, it automatically means that the balance sheet has to come into play in order to secure that growth. So, as of the quarter end, PFLT had a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.6x, which is way above the sector average of 1.19x.

Granted, the Management commented in the recent earnings call that subsequent to quarter end, it sold around $300 million of assets to its joint ventures, which has reduced the leverage level to 1.4x (frankly speaking, it is still quite high).

And here comes the problem.

In the earnings report, PFLT announced that it has formed and capitalized a new unconsolidated JV in order to drive the NII per share results up, while de-risking the balance sheet. As I articulated in my recent article - The Hidden Risk In The BDC Market That Nobody Is Talking About - it is a pattern that we can see more and more happening since most BDCs have a) exhausted dividend coverage levels, b) exhausted debt capacity levels, c) share prices below NAV, which limits equity-driven growth, and d) they are facing some serious headwinds from lower SOFR. So, to somehow fight back the pressures and at least preserve the NII levels, BDCs have to expand their portfolios.

Taking the unconsolidated JV route is virtually the only realistic option for BDCs to secure that. But what it means is that BDCs are just transferring their assets to these unconsolidated JVs and receiving cash for that, which enables debt reduction. However, the JVs themselves can take (and actually do take) much higher leverage levels than what is allowed per the BDC regulation. In a nutshell, this process transfers leverage from a visible place (i.e., BDC consolidated financials) to an invisible JV, where we can easily assume that the debt loads are significantly more aggressive than at the BDC-level.

Now, let's put these unconsolidated JVs into the right context.

As of September 30, PFLT's portfolio reached $2.8 billion. But, for PFLT's JV (PSSL), the total asset base, as of September 30, stood at $1.1 billion. This is what the Management has commented on its plan pertaining to PSSL:

We believe that the increase in scale of PSSL's balance sheet will continue to drive attractive mid-teens return on invested capital and enhanced PFLT's earnings momentum.

In other words, PSSL's balance sheet will very likely continue to expand.

The Management has also provided some color on what it expects to get from its newly formed PSSL II:

Our game plan is to grow PSSL 2 to be in excess of $1 billion in assets similar to our existing joint ventures.

Sooner or later, we could expect that PFLT will have more assets in its unconsolidated JVs than what it directly owns on its consolidated books.

Where I can give credit to PFLT is that it does a really good job of being transparent about the capital structures and performance metrics of these JVs.

So, if we look at the 10-K report, we can notice that PSSL's asset base is indeed $1.1 billion. We can also see that the total debt is ~$1.0 billion. Namely, it is very, very aggressively leveraged.

The bottom line

All in all, I have decided to fully exit PFLT, while still locking in some positive profits. The aggressive, hidden leverage-driven growth strategy introduces way too high risks, which are not really offset by PFLT's high-quality portfolio.

Plus, the fact that the dividend is seriously underwater (coverage level of 88%) and the consolidated leverage is significantly above the sector average (despite all of the support from debt deconsolidation), does not help at all the overall situation.

In my view, the chances are not that low that in the short to medium term, PFLT manages to deliver NII per share growth as it levers up the PSSL and newly established PSSL II JVs. However, I doubt that it will work this way in the longer term. PFLT would have to recognize a couple of non-accruals to really experience significant NAV erosion and income drop from the JV sources (due to the magnified effects from the abnormal leverage).

data.title: PayPal's Gloom Is Overdone: Prepare For The 2026 Comeback Story
data.description:
PayPal: 2025 Looks Like Another One To Forget

It's truly a year to forget for long-suffering investors of PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL). The recovery in 2024 proved to be short-lived, as investors reassessed whether they could have been too ambitious with the valuation re-rating in PYPL, given the transformative changes that are undergoing in the fintech and payments marketplace.

It already had much to deal with, as it wrangles with the increasing slate of competitors vying for market share. These rivals run the gamut, ranging from big tech with walled garden credentials like Apple (AAPL), to encroaching e-commerce rivals seeing payments as a natural extension to their market expansion, such as Amazon (AMZN) and Shopify (SHOP).

And then we have core marketplace rivals such as Klarna, and Stripe, as they battle for market leadership. PayPal notched a relatively constructive growth in BNPL in the third quarter (+20% upside in BNPL TPV). Yet, it remains to be seen whether margin optimization in PayPal's earnings could be enough to help rein in the industry headwinds that have finally decoupled PYPL's confident ascent back in 2024, in contrast to the malaise that investors have had to manage this year, with the stock down >30% over the past year.

Back in October, I kept my faith in PYPL stock, thinking that the relative undervaluation was a disconnect and that the market had taken PYPL's fall way too far. I couldn't quite fathom why the market turned so drastically against the fintech leader's recovery in 2025, even as the overall market has continued to print new highs. However, it appears that these investors could have gotten it right on point here, following the recent conference that PayPal partook in, as CFO & COO Jamie Miller unveiled some worrying signs about the current state of consumer spending and sentiments.

PayPal: Consumer Headwinds Increasingly Somber

PayPal will look to invest "1 to 2 points of transaction margin dollars back" into the business, as it seeks to effectuate more robust consumer adoption and what it terms "habituation." I think this is something that could have surprised investors who have keenly followed PYPL's thesis for a while. Why?

Isn't PayPal a solid enough household name that it should have used 2024 to consolidate and then capitalized on 2025 as a year of revival to wind down those less profitable setups? Yet, management's commentary indicates that we might have to wait till 2026 or even longer, plausibly to ascertain whether the recovery is still panning out as anticipated? No good....clarity is now more murky, and investors are made to wait longer for the turnaround thesis.

Next, PayPal highlighted that stress sensors on consumer spending are starting to bite, one that I acknowledge that I should have paid more attention to. Such a cagey commentary amid what seems like a robust Black Friday shopping weekend suggests that PayPal's exposure to the lower and middle income could set back its outlook for 2026 when the company reports Q4 earnings early next year.

Management enunciated that the cautionary signals have led to a clear trend in "lower average order value," which has also impacted the accretion in its core branded checkout prospects. The discrepancy between the less affluent consumers and their wealthier peers has also led to fears about the "affordability crisis," and whether it could lead to adverse political ramifications when the midterms come into play next year.

While these conjectures remain speculative, there is nothing along the lines of guesswork here when we take into account the clear pessimism reflected in the gloomy looking consumer sentiments index, printing levels that have deviated close to historic lows away from the long-term average. And with recent jobs reports not looking exactly sanguine, PayPal's business impact could linger on longer through 2026, even though a hard landing isn't my base case at the moment.

PayPal: Yet, Fundamentals Stay Robust And Resolute

Yet, the salient question that I thought investors should ask is whether PayPal's earnings profile is going to slide down to new lows. From what I had gathered from PayPal's conference, it doesn't appear to be the case. Management highlighted its record in delivering robust free cash flow of $6B to $7B in the past couple of years, while having also declared a dividend back in Q3. Therefore, I thought PayPal's decision to invest more aggressively isn't predicated on weakness, but a show of strength in its underlying financials and the outlook through 2026.

Nevertheless, I'm not about to argue with the market and make the clarion call that the market is wrong. As investors/analysts, we should always maintain humility in our analysis and assessment, and try to glean useful insights from the market's perspective into PayPal's afflictions.

I think the market could be baking in more trouble brewing ahead for PayPal's consumer and merchant ecosystem, if the unemployment and jobs reports continue to flag increased risks, worsening the already torrid-looking consumer sentiment updates.

Yet, I think the silver lining here is that PayPal's robust FCF through 2027 (>$7.5B) provides credence to my narrative that these tough times shall pass, as long as the Fed plays ball through the end of 2026. With the current National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett lining up as the hot favorite to replace Jerome Powell as the next Fed chair, I think we can suggest that Hassett should be broadly aligned with Trump's penchant for lower interest rates.

I think the market will likely skew in favor of a less hawkish Fed than worrying about one where the Fed's independence could be eroded. The market has reasons to believe that the FOMC is beyond one man/woman's decision or proclivities, even though raised dissent could also send shockwaves through the market. Despite that, the market still expects the Fed to cut rates by >3x through the end of 2026, which could help provide some helpful scaffolding for the pessimistic consumer, as merchants prepare cautiously for the effects to percolate through their businesses next year.

Buy Or Sell PYPL Stock?

If we dissect the buying sentiments in PYPL's long-term chart, I believe it's sufficient for me to say that while the underperformance has been somewhat frustrating, there's a robust support level over the $55 zone. It's noteworthy that the level has held resoundingly since early 2024, although little did I expect the market to make a round trip back close to those levels through November 2025.

However, if we take into account the fact that PYPL's forward earnings multiple of 11x is even lower than the financial sector's (XLF) forward P/E of 16x, has the market turned too downcast for its own good? PayPal is still printing 438M active accounts as of Q3, augmenting my narrative that its sticky ecosystem isn't showing glaring signs of falling apart. It has also rounded up the less profitable businesses in unbranded, and charted a more aggressive path in its core branded opportunities.

While the recent partnerships in Agentic commerce with Perplexity and OpenAI may not yet yield clear results in the near term, they also show that PayPal is acting quickly to seize the initiatives with the leading LLM companies, while walled garden peers like Amazon dither in favor of their in-house tech.

PayPal's ecosystem could be further diversified, as it attempts to bridge the gaps of its relatively stagnant active accounts base (but still sticky) with the rapidly growing 800M+ active users that OpenAI reportedly accrued.

Therefore, I believe dip buyers should be looking to return more favorably as it inches closer to that $55 support level, after realizing that such steep pessimism doesn't seem justified after all. Of course, a worse consumer outlook through 2026 could set investors back further, but it's not as if the current sentiments aren't bad enough already, skewing the risk/reward in favor of a reversal from here.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: My Portfolio Strategy For 2026
data.description:
I fear that we could be facing a lost decade.

Valuations are today at the highest levels ever recorded, according to a combination of various valuation metrics:

At the same time, we are facing unprecedented levels of uncertainty with the biggest war since WWII waging in Europe for its 4th year, even as China is at high risk of invading Taiwan, possibly already in the coming years.

Meanwhile, while the AI revolution offers great long-term promises, I expect a painful transition period as increasingly many people lose their jobs and struggle to find a new one. We are just now starting to see this reflected in massive layoff announcements:

  1. UPS: 48,000 employees
  2. Amazon: Up to 30,000 employees
  3. Intel: 24,000 employees
  4. Nestle: 16,000 employees
  5. Accenture: 11,000 employees
  6. Ford: 11,000 employees
  7. Novo Nordisk: 9,000 employees
  8. Microsoft: 7,000 employees
  9. PwC: 5,600 employees
  10. Salesforce: 4,000 employees
  11. Paramount: 2,000 employees
  12. Target: 1,800 employees
  13. Kroger: 1,000 employees
  14. Applied Materials: 1,444 employees
  15. Meta: 600 employees.

(Source: The Kobeissi Letter.)

We are also rapidly approaching $40 trillion in debt, with unprecedented levels of deficit. But what we spend today will inevitably have an impact on the future, as all of this debt will need to be serviced, hurting future budgets:

Despite already hitting unprecedented levels of deficit, the current administration is talking about potentially removing income taxes, sending everyone a “$2,000 tariff dividend,” and cutting interest rates down to 1%, all while fighting trade wars with the rest of the world, including allies.

Finally, we are long overdue for a lost decade. They are much more common than most investors realize, and with so many potential significant headwinds (high valuations, war, painful AI transition, overleverage, irresponsible fiscal, monetary, and trade policy, etc.), I think that the risk of one occurring rises significantly:

So how do you position yourself for such an environment?

In what follows, I am going to lay out my portfolio strategy and asset allocation going into 2026.

But before that, let me first remind you that there is no one solution that fits all. Each investor has different return objectives, risk tolerance, and other personal circumstances. Therefore, what I am doing may not make sense for you, and vice versa.

With that in mind, here is my portfolio right now:

Real Assets: 50%

Legendary investor and CEO of private equity powerhouse Brookfield (BAM), Bruce Flatt, believes that real assets are ideally positioned for this environment. Here is what he said in a recent letter to his investors:

"Over the past 15 years, governments have responded to successive economic slowdowns with large fiscal programs. The global financial crisis, the sluggish growth of the mid-2010s, and the pandemic each led to expansive interventions. Together, those interventions contributed to a very significant buildup of public debt. The combination of large, ongoing government deficits and “higher” interest rates has made this fiscal trajectory increasingly unsustainable...

For policymakers, there are a few paths to stabilize the debt burden. The most constructive outcome would be faster economic growth that outpaces the growth of debt, allowing leverage ratios to decline naturally over time... A second alternative path is reduced fiscal spending, but the political appetite for austerity seems to have dramatically diminished across much of the developed world. As a result, broad adoption of fiscal responsibility does not seem likely. The third alternative for policymakers is to quietly manage interest rates below inflation and gradually reduce debt burdens. We are already seeing this dynamic play out across the globe. Central banks are employing forms of yield-curve control and balance-sheet expansion to ensure that debt servicing remains manageable—Japan has done this for decades, and both China and parts of Europe more recently. In essence, short rates are going to be taken down and long rates will be “twisted” down without the system breaking to alleviate the interest burden. In the event that governments pursue this path, the likely result will be a period of declining real yields and low-ish nominal rates. This will create a different investment climate than that which we have experienced in recent years.

This environment provides optimal conditions for the real assets in which we invest, offering inflation-linked cash flows backed by hard assets that protect real returns. The benefits of real assets are always evident, but in this evolving environment they become an essential part of an investment portfolio. A suppression of real yields will further amplify these benefits—specifically, lower rates will further enhance cash flows by reducing financing costs." [emphasis added]

I agree with him, and I am positioning my portfolio accordingly.

The one difference in my approach is that I am mostly targeting listed real asset companies, such as REITs (VNQ), in the public market.

They are today trading at their lowest valuations ever relative to equities, with many trading at 30, 40, or even up to 50% discounts relative to the value of the real estate they own.

The last time REITs were this cheap was following the pandemic crash, and Bruce Flatt himself back then said the following:

"Probably the greatest discount out there between what you would see as value and price is in REITs and real estate securities... I would say one of the great purchases today is real estate securities because you are buying them at a fraction of what you would trade them at in the private sector... REITs that have high-quality assets trade at enormous discounts to the tangible value of their assets."

Today is no different. In fact, Bruce Flatt, via Brookfield, just recently offered to acquire one of my REITs, National Storage REIT (NSR / OTC:NTSGF), in a massive $6 billion transaction at a 26% premium to its latest share price. Even at that price, he would be getting a bargain in my opinion, which shows just how cheap REITs have gotten.

My real asset strategy focuses primarily on essential infrastructure that our society desperately needs, cannot be replaced by technology, and is recession-resistant. I then seek to invest at a large discount to my estimate of fair value.

Good examples include:

  • Apartment communities in supply-constrained markets via UDR (UDR). People will always need a roof over their heads, rents continue to grow in UDR’s markets, and yet, it trades at an estimated 30% discount to its net asset value.
  • E-commerce warehouses in growing sunbelt markets via EastGroup Properties (EGP). The AI revolution will only accelerate the trend of e-commerce, increasing the demand for EGP’s properties, but this is not yet reflected in its valuation.
  • Self-storage via CubeSmart (CUBE). I think that the coming labor market disruption will lead to greater mobility and demand for self-storage. Even then, the company is currently priced at a 25% discount relative to the private market value of its assets, net of debt.

These are just a few examples among many others. My point is that REITs, in many cases, now allow you to invest in real assets at lower valuations and with the added benefits of liquidity, diversification, and professional management. This is why even the likes of Bruce Flatt are now increasingly going after REITs.

They are unlikely to deliver NVIDIA-like (NVDA) returns, but many should be able to deliver 10%+ annual total returns even with modest assumptions, simply between their ~5% dividend yield and ~5% annual growth.

That becomes very compelling if we are to face a lost decade.

Select Equities: 40%

I am very selective with my traditional equity investments because I fear that most businesses will ultimately suffer from the AI revolution as it breaks barriers to entry in nearly every industry, and will inevitably lead to much greater competition and lower margins. I believe that it will prove highly deflationary and will result in lower profits for most companies. Besides, most of the market is priced at fairly aggressive valuations even pre-AI disruption.

But there are exceptions that will benefit from this, and I am focusing heavily on those.

Good examples include:

  • Blue Owl Capital (OWL) is one of the leading asset managers focusing on data centers. I expect its assets under management to grow at a rapid pace as massive investments keep pouring into data centers, and yet, it trades at a 6% dividend yield.
  • HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital, Inc. (HASI) finances renewable energy projects, which are essential to power the rapidly rising global energy consumptions. It has guided to grow its FFO per share by 8-10% annually going forward, and yet, it still offers a 5% dividend yield.
  • International Workplace Group (IWG / OTCPK:IWGFF) is the leading provider of co-working spaces, much bigger even than WeWork. I expect the office market to undergo a transformation over the coming decade, with increasingly many tenants requesting more flexible office lease terms as a result of the move towards hybrid work settings and the AI revolution causing white-collar labor market disruption. I expect IWG to be the biggest winner in this as it partners with landlords to manage their flexible office space in exchange for rapidly growing fee income.

In short, I only invest in select businesses with clear secular growth tailwinds that the market has not yet recognized. My equity portfolio is intentionally highly concentrated as I fear that the majority of businesses could suffer significant headwinds over the coming decade.

Cash & Alternatives: 10%

Finally, I hold enough cash to sleep well at night and some investments in alternative assets for extra diversification. This includes a start-up investment, a VC fund, private credit, and land investments.

Closing Note

As noted in the introduction, my approach may not work for you, but given my outlook and my personal circumstances, it makes sense for me.

If the big tech bull market carries on, I will likely underperform. But if and when that bubble deflates, I would expect my portfolio to significantly outperform, and it would do so with much lower risk and greater income.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Extreme Networks: Margin Expansion And SaaS Acceleration, Undervalued Alpha In AI Networking
data.description:
My analysis is leading me to assign a Buy rating for Extreme Networks (EXTR) stock. The core reason behind my bullish stance rests on Extreme Networks’s progressive shift toward a subscription-based, software-driven business model. By progressive shift I mean, there is a 24% Y/Y growth in SaaS ARR to $216 million and a total recurring revenue mix marking 36%. Extreme Networks is capitalizing on a specific market window formed by the consolidation-led disruptions of its larger competitors. I am specifically pointing to the Cisco partner program overhaul and the HPE-Juniper integration. This competitive displacement is materializing in big wins like the Fortune 86 heavy equipment manufacturer and major government contracts in the APAC region. Extreme Networks has managed prior inventory corrections and is now projecting an acceleration to double-digit revenue growth (10%) for FY2026 (remainder part). This growth may be coming from the product cycle involving Wi-Fi 7 and the AI-centric Extreme Platform ONE.

However, this rating is linked to specific risk on the gross margin recovery. Non-GAAP gross margins are guided to recover to the 63% range by FY2026’s end. Extreme Networks is currently countering headwinds from component cost increases (memory and optics) and shifts in product mix. Also, EXTR stock valuation depends heavily on the progressive upsell of the new Platform ONE subscription tiers to the existing install base. Failure to maintain the 95%+ platform attach rates and a slowdown in the Universal Hardware adoption can derail the margin expansion argument. Additionally, macro pressure on enterprise IT spending is still a latent threat. As a positive, Extreme Networks’ growing exposure to non-cyclical government and healthcare verticals builds a partial hedge. At the time of writing, the current stock price of ~$17.41 is a tactical entry point amid Extreme Networks’s expanding FCF profile and bottom-line growth potency.

24% SaaS ARR Growth Targets 100–200bps Margin Expansion in FY2026

As per my observations, the first catalyst for scaling Extreme Networks' stock price upward is the accelerating compounding of SaaS ARR and its direct correlation to margin expansion. In Q1-FY2026, total revenue grew 15% Y/Y, SaaS ARR grew at a faster pace of 24% (reaching $216 million). This is a profitability lever. Subscription and support gross margins are decisively higher than hardware margins. As the SaaS ARR grows to be a larger %-age of the total revenue mix (now 36% recurring), it can scale up the blended corporate gross margin upward (mathematically). The FY2026 guidance projects gross margins expanding toward 63% (+100-200 basis points).

This expansion is structural, as every incremental dollar of revenue brings-in disproportionately higher earnings and FCF. The market has historically undervalued Extreme as a hardware box-pusher, in my opinion. If the topline mix shift permeates the financials, the market can re-rate the stock (on a forward basis) with a higher P/E multiple in line with software-hybrid comps. Along with that, the second core catalyst is the Platformization strategy (Extreme Platform ONE). I am observing a distinct shift in the client purchasing pattern. 59% of clients are now buying the full stack (switching+wireless+software), and 90% of bookings from partners involve the full stack. So why is it a critical forward point here? Because full-stack clients have higher lifetime value (LTV) and lower churn than single-product clients.

Platform ONE is simplifying licensing into a single tier. Therefore, it is reducing sales friction and accelerating ‘land and expand’ motions. As pointed out in the Analyst/Investor Day call, early adopters of Platform ONE are having capacity gains of up to 300K hours/year through AI automation. This ROI point for the clients creates a pricing advantage for Extreme, logically. Extreme Networks is transforming the network from a utility into a value-added service by integrating AI agents for troubleshooting and network management directly into the subscription. This integration is justifying price increases and securing renewals. The capability to capture 10%-15% Average Contract Value (ACV) uplift through the platform sales can be the direct driver of forward topline beats.

Beyond that, the third core bullish catalyst is the competitive displacement cycle under the Cloud Choice. Opposite to cloud competitors who force clients into a public cloud, Extreme Networks provides public, private, and on-premises cloud management with the same feature set. This is an important advantage point amid the Sovereign Cloud opportunity in heightened geopolitical tension and data sovereignty laws (like GDPR in Europe). The win with the Japanese government and the deployment at Kroger (28K stores) is the observable basis here. The capability to win these large enterprise/government deals from incumbents like Cisco is a strong signal. The disruption at Cisco (partner program changes) and HPE/Juniper (merger integration) has led to a temporal dislocation in the channel. Extreme is capitalizing on this dislocation by engaging with disenfranchised partners looking for stable alternatives. The 2x Y/Y growth in Managed Service Provider (MSP) bookings indicates that channel partners are actively migrating workloads to Extreme Networks.

Technicals and Modeling on EXTR Stock Projects 67% Price Upside by 2026 and 162% by FY2029

To expand, my technical assessment for Extreme Networks stock indicates that after facing a resistance of ~$22.84 (dotted-horizontal red), the stock price has reverted downwards. The stock price is now below the pivot (dashed-horizontal yellow) and the price channel (dotted-horizontal yellow). For me, there is a correction going on, and a downward trend is in force.

On the upside, projecting the uptrend (initiated in June 2024) forward based on the golden ratio Fibonacci extension and under a breakout above the yellow price channel, the stock price may end up at ~$26.2 (by H1-2026) and ~$29.02 (by 2026’s end) following the diagonal higher-lows trendlines (parallel light-grey). The stock is currently moving at the support level of ~$17.56.

On the downside, the stock can take support on Fibonacci retracements based on the golden ratio at ~$15.20 (by H1-2026) and ~$11.38 (by 2026’s end) following the diagonal lower-highs trendlines (parallel dark-grey). Overall, there is a max technical upside of 67% and downside of 34.5% in the next 12 months. Through the Dollar-Average Method on these accumulation and profit-booking price levels, price return profile can be optimized at max on long-positions.

Fundamentally, the state of cash flows builds a floor for the stock price, possibly can fuel upward momentum. Extreme Networks had an operating cash flow of $152 million for FY2025. This is a big increase from prior years. This cash flow led to a $200 million share repurchase program authorization (as of July 1). In Q1 alone, Extreme Networks repurchased 577K shares (Q1 transcript). Share buybacks in Extreme Networks case serve a dual purpose. One is that they provide technical support for the stock and mathematically increase EPS by reducing the denominator. With a net cash position of $7.8 million (after considering debt) and growing FCF, Extreme Networks has the flexibility (in balance sheet) to continue stock buybacks (or pursue tuck-in acquisitions) without dilution effect.

On the Rule of 40 (Revenue Growth + Profit Margin), Extreme approaches this through combined growth and EBITDA expansion (adjusted EBITDA annualized at ~$180 million). I consider this as a lighthouse for big guys/institutional investors to identify EXTR as a viable target. This can drive up stock volume and price on a forward basis. Along with that, the deferred revenue of $618 million (Q1-transcript) is a base for forward earnings, as this backlog is a contractually committed revenue that has not yet been recognized by Extreme Networks. A 10% Y/Y increase in total deferred recurring revenue boosts high stability into forward quarters. This stability reduces the volatility risk (in valuations) associated with hardware sales cycles.

Therefore, the growing deferred topline and the stabilizing hardware backlog mark that Extreme Networks is progressively converting one-time hardware buyers into long-term software subscribers. If this deferred revenue transmits into the income statement over the next quarters, it may drive a tailwind for quarterly earnings beats. These beats will be the catalyst for short-term stock price appreciation towards the technical profit booking levels.

I want to compute the intrinsic value of Extreme Networks stock through a relative valuation model. For multiple expansion adjustments, I am using Current P/E (non-GAAP forward) of ~17.29x (based on the $1.01 FY2026 EPS consensus estimate midpoint). Competitors with similar SaaS/Hardware mix (like Arista and HPE-Juniper) often trade at ~25x P/E (on average, ANET and HPE, EXTR PE are making swing highs here too) over 5 years. If Extreme progressively executes the SaaS transition (like growing ARR over 20%), the market may re-rate the multiple to 25x. Projecting the PE over the consensus EPS estimates for the next 4 years indicates a 161.8% price upside. These levels, $25.25 and $29, are closely in line with the technical Profit Booking Levels.

Gross Margin Risk Can Cut EPS and Drive Stock Toward $11.38 if Supply Costs Outpace Pricing Power

Within the main forward factors, there are major risks too that I want to point out. The core stock price downside risk may come from gross margin compression amid supply chain volatility. As per FY2025 10-K, Extreme Networks depends on sole-source providers for merchant silicon (mainly Broadcom) and memory chips. Extreme Networks already had to implement a price increase to offset rising component costs (memory and optics). On a forward basis, if these input costs rise faster than Extreme Networks can pass them on to clients and if price increases impact demand elasticity, gross margins may fail to meet the 62%+ guidance mark. This has already happened, as seen in gross margin contraction in Q1 (61.3%) against the previous year (63.7%). If this trend continues instead of reversing as guided, the market will rerate the stock downwards for failing to realize the expected software-mix benefits.
