Along with that, an execution failure in the Platformization strategy can be considered very bad for the stock price. For instance, if Platform ONE fails to gain traction and, similarly, if the AI agents (hallucinations and reliability risks) cause client dissatisfaction, the churn can spike. Extreme Networks is progressing heavily on moving clients from perpetual licenses to subscriptions. If this transition creates a J-curve where revenue dips before it grows, and If Extreme Networks gets stuck in the dip (like the hardware sales slowdown because clients are waiting for new subscription models, but subscription uptake is sluggish), revenue growth can stall and can turn negative. Currently, Seeking Alpha Quant system puts a 'C' label on forward revenue growth, as growth has been average (not exceptional!) relative to the sector.

Also, macroeconomic issues affecting the Mid-Market is an additional risk against my bullish argument. Extreme is moving upmarket; however, a large portion of its TAM ($10 billion) (from 10-K) remains in the mid-market (500-999 employees). This segment is highly sensitive to rates and credit availability. If the global economy slows, these clients will likely be the first to delay network refreshes, in my opinion. The Inventory line item in the balance sheet had a decrease ($93.7 million vs. $102.6 million in the prior quarter). This is a positive, but Extreme Networks recorded large Excess and Obsolete inventory charges in FY24 ($64.5 million total). I am just pointing out the past difficulties in forecasting demand accurately.

So, if demand softens again, further inventory write-downs can materially impact EPS. These factors may drive the stock price down toward the support levels of $11.38. If these issues become worse than expected, there can be operational stumbles that turn FCF negative and can immediately hurt the balance sheet. I am enlisting this here because it can potentially force a suspension of the stock buyback. The removal of the buyback support (technical floor) may result in a sharp decline in the stock price (even below $11.38).

__

Overall, my stance on Extreme Networks stock is to give it an accumulate/buy rating. However, I will closely track three specific metrics in the upcoming quarters. One is the SaaS ARR Growth that must remain above 20% Y/Y. Any deceleration here breaks the growth rationale. The second one is the track of gross margin progression toward 62% in Q2FY26. Any failure to expand margins indicates pricing power weakness/supply chain leakage. Last monitorable is the FCF conversion, any large divergence in FCF and the operating income can mark working capital issues (likely inventory build-up).

data.title: TSMC: An Underrated Gem In The AI Chip Era
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

While the global data center market is estimated to grow to about $652.01 billion by 2030 at a CAGR of 11.2% between 2025 and 2030, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) is positioning itself to benefit from this opportunity. It is doing so by manufacturing the silicon brains, behind it all—chips for Nvidia, AMD, Apple, and Broadcom—that I think will make or break the next wave of intelligence.

TSM is the world’s leading pure-play foundry, commanding over 90% of advanced node production, yet its shares hover just around $290 following a 48.3% YTD surge, beating the S&P 500’s 16.6%.

As hyperscalers like Google and Meta scramble for capacity, one might wonder whether TSM is the essential pick-and-shovel enabler of the AI boom, or a geopolitical powder keg trading at a 30x PE! Given its strong financials and the massive market opportunity coupled with its solid market position, I am bullish on this stock and rate it a buy.

The Qualitative Take

TSM has led a revolution in the tech industry by separating chip design from manufacturing, thus allowing fabless innovators like Nvidia to focus on innovation while outsourcing production. I am simply discussing TSM as the essential enabler in the AI wave. Today, this company operates several advanced fabs across Asia, Europe, and North America, producing over 12 million 12-inch equivalent wafers annually. The company’s business model's core strength is its scale. Serving more than 500 customers with processes from mature 350nm nodes to the advanced 2nm, generating more than $88 billion in revenue in 2024.

But why is TSM so important? I will highlight two major aspects that make this company a very critical enabler. I will begin by mentioning its leadership in process nodes, where 3nm is in volume production and 2nm is on track for 2025. Further, A16 at 1.6 nm has timelines of late 2026. Each node is estimated to deliver about 15% higher performance gains, about 24-35% power reduction, and a higher transistor density, translating to cooler and more efficient AI training and inference chips. Secondly, its CoWoS advanced packaging (CoWoS-L, CoWoS-R, and the upcoming CoWoS-S, which has more than 1 TB/s interposers) allows high multi-die systems more chips per package. This solves the bandwidth and yield problem that would otherwise choke the next-gen AI superchips.

With these distinctive features, I am looking at TSM against its competitors Samsung and Intel, who are one to two nodes behind with lower yields. As such, I see TSM as not just a supplier but an essential bottleneck and moat that every major AI player must secure. In my view, this explains why Apple has secured half of TSM’s 2nm chip production capacity for its iPhone 18. Further, the company’s financials are a testament to its competitive market position.

In Q3 2025, for example, its High Performance Computing [HPC] segment grew by 57% YoY to about $18.9 billion, accounting for 57% of the total revenue. I am not surprised, and I don’t see this as hype. Nvidia’s Blackwell GPUs, AMD’s MI300 series, and the custom silicon for Amazon and Google all rely on TSM’s advanced nodes. In my view, this explains why advanced technologies (7 nm and below) accounted for about 74% of the wafer revenue in Q3 2025.

While I acknowledge the US-China tensions, I find TSM’s $65 billion Arizona investment, which is now ramping up to $100 billion, as a smart move to counter the possible threat from these geopolitical tensions. This expansion in the US alongside its European expansion signals an important shift that diversifies from Taiwan’s 80% capacity share. With the company’s dominance in the foundry industry and with the most advanced AI chip makers relying on it, I think TSM is well aligned to seize the AI chip market, which is estimated to grow by a CAGR of 28.94% between 2025 and 2034, to a market size of $931.26 billion.

The Numbers: Financials & Valuation

When I look at this company’s financials, I find it very robust and attractive. Let me take it from the Q3 2025 results. The company reported revenue of $33.1 billion, a YoY growth of 40.8%, and beat estimates by about $1 billion. This was driven by 74% of wafer revenue from advanced nodes.

Notably, it marked the 7th successive quarter of more than 30% YoY revenue growth, which is a testament to its strong revenue momentum and demand for its products. Profitability was also attractive, with net profit margin coming in at 45.7%, a 2.9 bps growth YoY.

From the TTM scale, revenue has grown close to $120 billion compared to FY2024 revenue of ~$88 billion. This strong growth is also evident in the net profit, which has improved from $35.26 billion in 2024 to $51.57 billion in the TTM. Comparatively, TSM is performing more excellently than its peers. Although its revenue base is lower than that of Samsung, primarily due to Samsung’s diverse business model, it is apparent that TSM’s revenue curve shows a steeper growth gradient, a testament to the fact that it is growing faster than the two competitors.

Most importantly, it has the highest gross and net margins, which signal its strong profitability compared to its peers. In my view, this strong profit margin is a testament to its unmatched pricing power due to its advanced nodes as well as its cost efficiency.

Moving to the balance sheet, TSM has solid liquidity, with cash and short-term investments standing at about $90 billion, the highest of the three companies. This speaks to its impressive liquidity, which offers the company financial flexibility to keep exploring new advanced nodes, as is the case with 1.6nm which is in the pipeline. To solidify its position is its low debt-to-EBITDA ratio, which is legible below 1 alongside that of Samsung. However, with TSM’s stronger liquidity, I believe its balance sheet stands out, which is a sign of its financial strength.

In terms of valuation, INTC has its PE ratio hovering in the north of 3800x due to its high losses experienced in its foundry business. This is a reflection of its weakness relative to TSM which is a pure foundry making solid profits. Compared to Samsung, which is trading at an 18.9x PE ratio, TSM’s PE of 30.02x could appear as a premium pricing. However, I think this is even an undervaluation at its current valuation.

I will look at it from two perspectives. I will begin by looking at the forward EPS growth rates and why Samsung's negligible forward EPS growth rate and INTC’s -17.2% are way lower than TSM’s 34.08% forward EPS growth. This testifies that this company’s strong profitability momentum is set to continue at an unmatched rate, which, in my view, calls for a market revaluation.

Another way of looking at it is by comparing it with Nvidia and AMD who rely on TSM for their advanced AI chips. Surprisingly, despite TSM being the inevitable enabler for their innovative products, TSM’s PE of ~30x is way lower than Nvidia’s PE in the north of 44x and AMD’s above 58x as of 5th December 2025. From this assessment, I believe the market is undervaluing the critical role TSM plays in the AI era, and for this reason, I expect a market rerating. I am targeting a PE of about 40x, which, alongside the consensus EPS of $12.49 in 2026, yields a target price of about $500 by the end of FY2026.

Risks

Geopolitical tensions

The company’s fabs are concentrated in Taiwan, making it acutely exposed to the escalating US-China tensions, which constitute a potential blockade or export restrictions that could disrupt global chip supplies. It should be noted that the US-China chip war in 2023 impacted TSM revenue, resulting in negative revenue growth in 2023. Even though the company is diversifying its global reach, which could solve this, the possible impacts of any negative developments cannot be underestimated.

Intensified CapEx

In order to maintain its node leadership, TSM is ramping up CapEx to record levels of about $40-$50 billion in 2026. This intensified outlay could lead to compressing margins should the company fail to sustain strong topline growth. While the market opportunity is massive and the company’s position in the market is very strong, any missteps here could lead to lower EPS and weaker market sentiments, making my target PE elusive.

Conclusion

At a time when AI is reshaping economies, TSM stands as an irreplaceable architect of this evolution by delivering chips that power tomorrow. There lies a massive market opportunity in the AI chip market, and with TSM’s competitive market position as the inevitable enabler, I reiterate my bullish stance and buy rating.
